nameMeanings.name: Yate
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: English: (i) locative name, occupational name for someone who lived by a gate (Middle English yat, Old English geat), in some cases because they acted as the gatekeeper or porter to a hall, a monastery, or some other important building. Redmonds, Dictionary of Yorks Surnames mentions a witness to a set of deeds who is variously named as John ad portam, John le Porter, and John atteyate, 1322–6 in Yorks Deeds (Wadworth, WR Yorks). Compare Yeatman , Gate locative name for someone who lived at or came from Yate (Gloucs).

variants.name: Yorks Deeds
meanings: English: relationship name from the Middle English personal name Duket. This may sometimes have been an early Middle English pet form of an Old English * Ducc(a) but was more often a pet form of Marmaduke, especially in Yorks ( See Duke ). Willelmus filius Duket of Hackness (NR Yorks) may have been related to Marmaduke de Sneaton, who lived in the neighbouring village of Sneaton (1301 in Subsidy Rolls ). Dukett de Thweng, 1416 in Yorks Deeds (Snaith, WR Yorks), was probably identical with or a relative of Marmaduke de Thweng, 1404 in Inquisitiones post Mortem (York).

variants.name: Yeatman
meanings: English (southern): occupational name for a gate-keeper, from Middle English yate + man. Compare Yates .

variants.name: Redmonds, Dictionary of Yorks Surnames
meanings: English: variant of Bulcock . Redmonds, Dictionary of Yorks Surnames notes that in the parish registers of Bolton by Bowland (WR Yorks) the same family name is spelled Bulcock, Bowcocke, and Boocock (1608–30), and that Edward Bowcock of Howgill (WR Yorks) is also called Bulcock in a will dated 1677. Compare also Alice Bowcock, 1641, Chr. Bulcock, 1646 in IGI (Barnoldswick, WR Yorks).

variants.name: Yates
meanings: English: from Middle English yates ‘gates’, plural of yate, Old English geat ‘gate’, hence a topographic or occupational name for someone who lived by the gates of a town or castle and who probably acted as the gatekeeper or porter. Compare Gates . Alternatively, a variant of the singular form Yate, with post-medieval excrescent -s.

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

en.lifeSummary: Mrs. William Yate was born in 1366, in Charney Bassett, Berkshire, England, United Kingdom. She had at least 1 son with William Yate. She died in her hometown.
